text: Hello, sharing a single port laparoscopic ileocolic resection in a young patient with Crohn's disease. Symptoms are primarily obstructive.
Patient had been on biologic therapy and recent steroids without any improvement, several admissions to the hospital.

The surgeon places a port one finger breadth above the pubic bone in the midline through a 2.5 cm transverse incision using a mini fan and steel instrument.
The surgeon elevates the ileocolic pedicle and performs a medial to lateral dissection to access the root of the pedicle, using careful manipulation to separate the tissue.
I do try to keep my single port access devices away from the peri-umbilical area due to the chance of hernia formation.
A case like this certainly is easier when the port is placed there. I find that this was a relatively easy, straightforward case.

The entire procedure and the dissection is exactly the same as it would be for a multiport laparoscopic case. The only difference being that the angle of view from the camera as well as the instrumentation is just a little bit different.

The surgeon completes the division of the ileocolic pedicle and performs a medial to lateral dissection, followed by a lateral dissection to further separate the tissue layers.
The surgeon mobilizes the colon around the hepatic flexure, preparing to exteriorize the specimen for a hand-sewn extracorporeal anastomosis through a fan and steel incision.

The only differences in this case is the view from the suprapubic port, as well as the fact that it's single port, so all your instruments are in the same port, which takes a little bit getting used to.

I think for people who are just beginning doing single port surgery, having the port peri-umbilical gives you a little bit more familiarity with the camera and maybe makes the dissection a little bit easier for you. I encourage, though, for everyone to try to do off midline and away from the peri-umbilical area incisions so that we decrease our hernia rate.
The surgeon mobilizes the root of the terminal ileum (TI) towards the duodenum, working underneath the ileocolic pedicle to facilitate the small bowel's reach to the port.
The surgeon utilizes a single port access device with two instruments visible: the Le Goucher and the Parkes Grasper, to perform the procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection, moving instruments back and forth. When smoke obscures the camera view, the surgeon pulls the camera into the port to clear the fog.
The surgeon joins the previously medially dissected space, visually connecting the current dissection area with the earlier dissected region.

I have done intracorporeal anastomosis single port. So if you're able even to do an extracorporeal anastomosis through that incision, you have still benefited the patient and the benefit of an intracorporeal isn't really there for the patient.

For the ones where I absolutely cannot get this to reach through the incision, I will do an intracorporeal anastomosis. And admittedly, sometimes I will have to add a five millimeter trocar around the umbilicus or just the left of the umbilicus for a different camera angle or instrumentation because it can be very challenging to do single port from this vicinity.
